title: Brown stew
subtitle: Beef and root vegetables slow-braised in one pot
excerpt: Brown stew is the workhorse of British country cooking: beef, root vegetables, and stock simmered until everything is tender and the gravy is thick and dark. It needs little skill, just time, and it improves overnight.
sourceNotes: Derived from the British farmhouse braise tradition. General method aligns with the approach described in Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, Project Gutenberg) for braised beef. Vegetable combination and modern quantities are adapted from contemporary British home-cooking practice.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely, then freeze in airtight containers for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently on the hob, adding a splash of water if the gravy has thickened.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well in a larger casserole. Brown the beef in batches so it sears rather than steams.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Make a day ahead, cool, refrigerate, and reheat on the hob over a low heat.

glossaryTerms.definition: Adding liquid to a hot pan to dissolve the browned deposits left by searing meat or vegetables, lifting their flavour into the sauce.

content.text: Brown stew is beef and root vegetables braised slowly until the meat is tender and the gravy is dark and thick. It gives you a proper, filling bowl of food with very little fuss: chop, brown, cover, wait. The slow oven does the work.
The only step that matters is getting a good colour on the meat before adding liquid. A pale braise tastes pale. Take your time with the browning and the rest follows.
You need a casserole dish with a tight-fitting lid, large enough to hold everything in a snug single layer. A 28 cm cast-iron casserole is ideal.
Heat the oven to 160°C conventional. Pat the beef dry with kitchen paper and season well with salt and pepper.
Warm the oil in the casserole dish over a high heat. Brown the beef in two or three batches, turning each piece so all sides are deep brown, about 3 minutes per batch. Lift out and set aside.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the onion to the same casserole and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the flour and stir for 1 minute until it coats the onion.
Add the tomato puree and cook for 1 minute, then pour in the stock,
the base as you stir. Add the Worcestershire sauce, bay leaves, and thyme sprigs.
Return the beef to the casserole along with the carrot and parsnip. The liquid should come about two-thirds up the sides of the meat. Add a splash more stock or water if not.
Cover with the lid and transfer to the oven. Cook for 2 to 2.5 hours until the beef is tender enough to break apart with a spoon. Check after 1.5 hours and add a little water if it looks dry.
Remove the bay leaves and thyme stalks. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve with mashed potato, crusty bread, or rice.

items.cause: The lid was left off, or the heat was too low to reduce the liquid.
items.fix: Lift out the beef and vegetables and simmer the gravy uncovered on the hob for 10 minutes until it thickens. Return everything to the pot.

items.symptom: The beef is tough after 2 hours
items.cause: Chuck needs time. It can take up to 3 hours depending on how cold it was when it went in.
items.fix: Return to the oven, covered, for another 30 to 45 minutes and test again.

items.symptom: The stew looks pale and tastes flat
items.cause: The beef was not browned enough at the start.
items.fix: This cannot be undone during cooking, but add a teaspoon of marmite stirred in at the end to deepen the colour and flavour.

items.cause: The root vegetables were cut too small, or the braise ran too long.
items.fix: Cut them no smaller than 3 cm for a 2.5-hour braise. If they are already soft, serve immediately; they will hold their shape less well on reheating.

content.text: Brown stew sits at the heart of British farmhouse cooking, a dish of frugal good sense: cheaper cuts of meat, whatever root vegetables were to hand, and a slow oven. It has no precise origin and no single recipe; every household had a version. What unites them is the dark, sticky gravy and the patience required to get there.
